corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Our algorithm makes use of two soft Q-functions to mitigate positive bias in the policy improvement step that is known to degrade performance of value based methods .

Although our algorithm can learn challenging tasks, including a 21-dimensional Humanoid, using just a single Q-function, we found two soft Q-functions significantly speed up training, especially on harder tasks.
Thus, we compute gradients for α with the following objective: The final algorithm is listed in Algorithm 1.
The algorithm is agnostic to the parameterization of the policy, as long as it can be evaluated for any arbitrary state-action tuple.
We compare our method to prior techniques on a range of challenging continuous control tasks from the OpenAI gym benchmark suite and also on the rllab implementation of the Humanoid task .
In this paper, we describe Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), our recently introduced off-policy actor-critic algorithm based on the maximum entropy RL framework.
In this paper, we describe Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), our recently introduced off-policy actor-critic algorithm based on the maximum entropy RL framework.
In this framework, the actor aims to simultaneously maximize expected return and entropy; that is, to succeed at the task while acting as randomly as possible.
We systematically evaluate SAC on a range of benchmark tasks, as well as challenging real-world tasks such as locomotion for a quadrupedal robot and robotic manipulation with a dexterous hand.

Unfortunately, the combination of off-policy learning and high-dimensional, nonlinear function approximation with neural networks presents a major challenge for stability and convergence .
This challenge is further exacerbated in continuous state and action spaces, where a separate actor network is often used to perform the maximization in Q-learning.

Finally, we present empirical results that show that Soft Actor-Critic attains a substantial improvement in both performance and sample efficiency over prior off-policy and on-policy methods including the recently introduced twin delayed deep deterministic (TD3) policy gradient algorithm .

Finally, we present empirical results that show that Soft Actor-Critic attains a substantial improvement in both performance and sample efficiency over prior off-policy and on-policy methods including the recently introduced twin delayed deep deterministic (TD3) policy gradient algorithm .

Our soft actor-critic algorithm incorporates three key ingredients: an actor-critic architecture with separate policy and value function networks, an off-policy formulation that enables reuse of previously collected data for efficiency, and entropy maximization to encourage stability and exploration.

Our soft actor-critic algorithm incorporates three key ingredients: an actor-critic architecture with separate policy and value function networks, an off-policy formulation that enables reuse of previously collected data for efficiency, and entropy maximization to encourage stability and exploration.

A particularly popular off-policy actor-critic method, DDPG , which is a deep variant of the deterministic policy gradient algorithm, uses a Q-function estimator to enable off-policy learning, and a deterministic actor that maximizes this Q-function.
As such, this method can be viewed both as a deterministic actor-critic algorithm and an approximate Q-learning algorithm.
Unfortunately, the interplay between the deterministic actor network and the Q-function typically makes DDPG extremely difficult to stabilize and brittle to hyperparameter settings .
